messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Provide a comprehensive analysis of the current state of the art, identify gaps, and explain how this project advances beyond existing knowledge.
Write the State of the art and innovation section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.2 Objectives Describe the objectives both for technology development and for business validation & development of the project. We have formulated a series of SMART (Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Relevant and Timebound) technology development and business validation and development objectives that will be fulfilled during the 24-month EIC Transition project. They are presented in Table 2, along with associated key performance indicators (KPIs) will be used for tracking progress. Table 2- S.M.A.R.T. project objectives for technology development and business validation and development Objective 1: To implement a cocreation approach with industry and standards/regulatory experts to accompany and systematically inform the technology development and business validation/development activities (to assist the Go-to-Market segmentation strategy) for industry and market success. KPIs: Experts from ≥ 20 industrial companies, OEMs, and stakeholders will participate in 4 scoping sessions by M6, not only from the application focus of this development, i.e. PVC and wider waste recycling, whereby we currently have Letters of Intent (LOIs) from Deceuninck, INEOS, Vynova, Veolia, PreZero & PICVISA. We also will have pathways to other industrial applications in the European process industries (A.SPIRE engaged with LOI, representing 170 industrial players & stakeholders from cement, ceramics, chemicals, engineering, minerals and ores, non-ferrous metals, pulp and paper, refining, steel & water sectors). A standards body (UNE from Spain is engaged). 1 industry partner will test and validate the VISUM LRS dual spectroscopic fusion analyser system in their PVC recycling facilities and provide feedback to guide optimisation and upscaling work (Deceuninck in their facility in Belgium); ≥ 40 industry and stakeholders (with assistance from ACCIO- Regional Development Agency of Catalonia- engaged with a LOI) will participate in demo days or in 1-to-1 presentations of the prototype demo unit. NTUA are also engaged with a LOI for future applications in mining & metallurgy. Objective 2: To perform laboratory experiments with PVC samples using hardware compatible Multirange Spectroscopic testbed that employs Machine Learning hybrid ensemble modelling to effectively implement the spectral data fusion. The most suitable optical fusion of spectroscopic techniques and their spectral sequence will be identified. Data generated during the experiments will effectively implement the spectral data fusion to development machine learning models that enhance the system’s predictive capabilities and to meet the performance KPIs defined by industry, market and regulatory requirements: ≥ 98% both accuracy and recall or alternatively Matthews Correlation Coefficient ≥ 0.97 in detecting the presence of 0.1% w/w and above of lead contaminants (e.g. B28 or TBLS lead stabilisers) and to differentiate the amount of phthalate plasticisers (DOP or DPHP) present in PVC samples. 1 publication of the results in an Open Access Journal. Technology Readiness Level: TRL4. Objective 3: To use the results of the experimental tests to inform the optimal downscaled designs of the dual spectroscopic LIBS-RS fusion analyser (along with any other techniques that may reveal themselves thanks to the multirange opportunity offered by the multirange spectroscopy testbed), and to build the device leveraging hardware compatibility. An interoperable data management platform will be developed for collecting and processing the multivariate data from the inspection device, and this data will be processed by the ML models for effectively implementing the data fusion in hybrid ensembling. The inspection device will be integrated in a prototype mechanical convey sorter, laboratory tests under controlled conditions simulation industrial environments using real production samples provided by our early adopters will confirm the achievement of the operational and economic KPIs defined by industry: Factory Acceptance Tests (FAT) confirm the inspection system is compatible with the typical industrial processor throughputs, i.e., conveyor belts running at 30 m/min; Affordability (market cost: <€125k). TRL4-5. Objective 4: To install the dual spectroscopic LIBS-RS fusion device and modular sorting system in an industrial PVC recycling facility, ensuring feasibility of implementation in a dynamic spot approach based on multipoint probing with a useful spatial resolution for industrial-grade challenges. Production trials will be performed to validate and demonstrate system accuracy, speed, and operational stability through exhaustive trials and feedback loops from industrial stakeholders. KPIs: Successful installation of the system in an industrial PVC recycling conveyor by M14, followed by processing trials demonstrating ≥ 90% both accuracy and recall or alternatively Matthews Correlation Coefficient ≥ 0.95 in detecting lead contaminants and detection capacity at convey belt speeds by M21. Reliability: Achieve >95% uptime during production trials pilot deployments. Site Acceptance Tests (SAT) and a validation report generated by M20, will capture system performance and operational feedback from the industry trials, which will be benchmarked against the pre-defined KPIs and will inform optimisations. Technology Readiness Level: TRL5-6. Objective 5: To optimise the prototype based on feedback from industry trials and develop a detailed roadmap for scaling the technology from TRL7 to TRL9, ensuring alignment with industrial-scale operational requirements and economic feasibility. KPIs: Completion of system optimisations based on trial feedback by M22, with demonstrated improvements in lead detection accuracy, processing speed, and operational efficiency. Development of a comprehensive scale-up and implementation roadmap (based on two implementation formats ‘dynamic spot’ for conveyor-based industrial applications and ‘static spot’ for non-conveyed samples to be inspected in an at-line setup), including costing, production, and deployment strategies, with input from the early adopters, by M24. Technology Readiness Level: Towards 6. Objective 6: To design a Go-to-Market strategy, including the development of a multi-tier business model that is verified with early adopters, our distribution network, and OEMs, and to ensure the necessary certifications for regulatory compliance are identified and fulfilled to pave the way for successful market entry. KPIs: Letters of Intent from 3 early adopters interested in post-project adoption (signed by M20); 80% of our global network of distributors have expressed interest in including the Visum LRS in their sales portfolios; 1 cooperation LOI signed with an OEM. Obtain necessary certifications (e.g., CE marking) (by M24). Business Validation & Development Objective. Objective 7: To develop a comprehensive business and financing plan to accelerate the post-project scale-up and commercialization of the dual spectroscopic fusion analyser. This plan will include a market feasibility study, market segmentation (to verify market entry application and to identify the roadmap of follow-on applications in conveyor and non-conveyor deployments), cost-benefit analysis, assessment of financial viability, market demand, regulatory costs, and projected returns on investment. An intellectual property (IP) strategy will also be included to protect innovations and strengthen market competitiveness. KPI: Completion of an investment-ready business and financing plan with integrated market feasibility, cost-benefit analysis, and IP strategy by M24 that includes a quantified financial model (CAPEX, OPEX, ROI, breakeven) aligned with realistic market uptake scenarios, is validated through external feedback (incl. ≥2 formal review sessions with investors, early adopters, or business support networks (e.g., EEN or relevant VCs). Business Validation and Development Objective. The project objectives are effectively designed to facilitate the development and validation of the dual LIBS-Raman spectroscopy analyser in real-world application environments: By engaging industry and regulatory experts from the outset in a cocreation approach, will ensure the technology is developed in alignment with market needs and compliance requirements. Through comprehensive laboratory experiments and testing, followed by integration into real industrial settings, the project’s phased approach guarantees that the system’s performance is validated in both controlled and operational environments. We will install the developed prototype in Deceuninck’s PVC recycling plant in Diksmuide, Belgium (signed LOI), which is one of the world’s most advanced PVC recycling facilities, with a capacity of 40,000 tonnes for rigid PVC (making it the largest of its kind in the Benelux). Building on our collaboration with Deceuninck in the REMADYL project, in which we were both consortium partners and whereby we worked with the team at the Diksmuide plant to identify potential optimal locations for the integration of the sorting system, in scoping sessions at the start of the EIC Transition project, we will prepare for seamless validation of the dual spectroscopic fusion analyser-driven sorting system in their facilities. Feedback from these industrial trials will inform optimisation work and subsequent development roadmap to align the technology with industrial-scale requirements, mitigating scalability risks. How the specific operational objectives address the challenges and unknown to bring the innovation to the public: The operational objectives have been defined based on our experience to date in scaling up and bringing to market fully certified CE mark (and ATEX compliance) real-time spectroscopy and Machine Vision (MV) analytical devices: Visum NIR In-Line™ - In-line NIR analyser; Visum Palm™ - Handheld NIR analyser; Visum HSI™- Hyperspectral Imaging System; Visum Raman In-Line™ - Raman analyser; Visum DeepSight™-MV System. Cocreation with a sufficient representation of industry applications and segments: waste recycling industry (plastics and PVC recycling), process industries (e.g. mining, chemical, food processing, bio-based industries, among others), and consultation with additional stakeholders, such as standards bodies, economic development agencies, industry association, among others, is a proven approach that has demonstrated itself to be successful in our developments to date. This is a proactive strategy for identifying 'unknowns' (so that hidden challenges related to deployment, scalability, and market needs can be uncovered early). This is very powerful in combination with a ‘top down’ methodology to tackling application development (enabled by our multirange spectroscopy testbed and hybrid ensembling modelling that is ideally suited to effectively delivering technological solutions to complex scenarios and ‘unknowns’. This, combined with an iterative approach to the development, allows us the adaptability to take account of learnings and findings to ensure that the product is well-prepared for real-world applications. By integrating business validation, certifications, and market analysis into the objectives, the project also tackles potential regulatory and commercial barriers, ensuring that the innovation is ready for public market entry, fully compliant, and economically viable for industry adoption. Methodology Approach and methodology Spectroscopic Benchmarking using a multirange Spectroscopic testbed (Visum ‘Fusion’): IRIS is an advanced engineering company specialising in the manufacture and integration of photonics-based, real-time process monitoring solutions. We have developed a competitive edge by staying technology-agnostic. As opposed to spectroscopy analyser vendors, our approach to solving complex Industry 4.0 monitoring challenges focuses on continually investigating and applying the most suitable techniques, driven by industrial requirements and application constraints as opposed to aligning with any single technique. Continually challenged with resolving industrial problems, we have been consistently pushing the boundaries of photonics- particularly spectroscopy- from an engineering (hardware) and scientific (physics) standpoint. This has culminated in the recent creation and development of a disruptive Multirange Spectroscopic testbed (called the Visum ‘Fusion’) that combines the principles of advanced spectroscopic (SWIR, NIR, MWIR, VIS, Raman, LIBS, Fluorescence, HSI) and MV techniques in a single platform. The techniques are seamlessly activated through shared spectrometers (Raman uses a VIS or a NIR spectrometer; LIBS and fluorescence use VIS), as well as common illumination systems (VIS, NIR and SWIR can all use a common halogen lamp, while lasers are required for LIBS and Raman), power supplies, software drivers and -in some cases- optical probes. This proprietary experimental unit works in combination with groundbreaking hybrid-ensemble modelling (discussed below) for implementing spectral data spectral data fusion to identify the most suitable technique combination thereof based on the parameters to be measured for each application. It serves as a unique experimental unit for the effective benchmarking of the distinct physics of a comprehensive range of spectroscopic and MV techniques. It is a powerful tool for tackling highly complex monitoring requirements & for easily exploring ‘unknowns’, thanks to its ubiquitous ability to ‘activate’ its comprehensive array of measurement techniques, the most effective combination of which is automatically orchestrated with the hybrid ensembling for implementing the correct spectral data fusion. In this way, we allow the solution ‘evolve’ to address the monitoring requirements in a top-down methodology, as opposed to experimenting with ‘predefined’ techniques, whereby we could miss the opportunity of addressing the ‘unknowns’. So, in this case, while the primary focus is on the LIBS-Raman fusion, the testbed will activate other techniques as required, especially in view both of the complexity of ‘old’ PVC waste processing and the high number of toxic additives used in plastics in low concentrations, requiring sensitive techniques for real-time detection in industrial recycling. Using the novel Hybrid Ensemble paradigm for performing seamless data fusion, to obtain dramatic improvements in precision, accuracy, and limit of detection: In machine learning (ML), "ensembling" means combining -according to certain predefined procedure- a plurality of different algorithms to be applied on a common raw dataset in order to take advantage of the particular capabilities of each algorithm for eventually generating a weighted output that takes into account the contribution from each one. Our hybrid ensembling approach extends this concept to the source of empirical data, i.e., different physics and technologies that contribute in different ways and extents to unveil the required information. Consequently, it entails widening the ensembling scope beyond the software possibilities by also involving the hardware that provides the raw data. In the same way that traditional ensembling improves the model capabilities by including several approaches in a common corpus, hybrid ensembling also includes different sources of multivariate data in an extended combination of algorithms and data types. There are several advantages associated to the hybrid ensembling concept, namely, increased data redundancy that renders more robust predictive models, higher multidimensional data spaces albeit lower collinearity risk because each technology explores different physical interactions, variables and technologies selection capabilities and improved model robustness and accuracy. Consequently, the ‘Fusion’ test bed will be used for developing the hybrid ensembling models by including both LIBS and Raman probes as well as other technologies according to a detailed design of experiments. Such a plan will comprehend the most relevant cases, as well as sufficient variety of ranges and circumstances to emulate the expected industrial conditions and needs. In terms of technological maturity, we have successfully implemented hybrid ensembling for optical data fusion in other applications, namely MERLIN and RECLAIM, whereby we have developed multi-model architectures that combined inputs from different sensors (spectroscopic and non-spectroscopic) to achieve reliable classification in complex material characterisation applications, specifically in waste plastic sorting, achieving, e.g. ≥94% accuracy classifying PET (polyethylene terephthalate), PP (polypropylene), PE (polyethylene), PS (polystyrene), PAPER-based, and non-ferrous METAL-based waste objects, using labelled laboratory datasets and structured multivariate cascade modelling (TRL3-4). We will apply this in the VISUM LRS case, and we will adapt and advance to TRL5-6, whereby models will be refined using new data from the VISUM Fusion testbed, retrained on industrial datasets from prototype integration, and optimised via online learning and feedback loops during real-world trials. This will ensure adaptation to process variability, speed, and material diversity. All models will be version-controlled, documented, and validated through cross-validation, statistical tests (e.g. Fisher-Pitman), and benchmarking, and their development will be aligned with the EC’s AI Act and Trustworthy AI principles (as discussed later in this section), ensuring robustness, traceability, and safe deployment in dynamic industrial environments. Downscaling concept- from the Fusion Testbed to a case-wise optimum set-up: The design and engineering of a prototype of the dual spectroscopic fusion analyser for on-line waste recycling will use a ‘downscaling’ process (fig.2) that is informed by the experimental work performed with the Multirange Spectroscopic testbed in conjunction with hybrid ensembling for obtaining information in relation to: the combination of spectroscopic techniques (LIBS, Raman and any other), the spectroscopic sequencing, as well as the ML Hybrid Ensembling models for implementing the spectral data fusion (leveraging the based on the PVC recycling use case). This downscaling process (i.e. translating the testbed to a case-wise optimum set-up) benefits from the common hardware compatibilities (i.e. the ‘Fusion Core’) of the testbed (fig. 2), i.e. ‘compatible-by-design’. To this end the dual spectroscopic fusion analyser will share common hardware, with specific settings tailored to each case: frequency-doubled Nd:YAG pulsed laser (that emits at 532 nm) can be shared by setting it at 2 two different pulse energy levels (lower for exciting Raman scattering and higher for generating the emitting plasma required for LIBS); the high spectral resolution spectrometer suitable for acquiring LIBS spectra is also compatible for acquiring Raman spectra as both phenomena take place in the VIS spectral range. In addition, common optical probes, power supply, trigger, and computing system can all be shared for cost reductions and compactness. The novel sequential spectral fusion approach—using both techniques in tandem, in two successive steps with minimal lag—can generate richer spectral data, which is ideal for leveraging data fusion techniques, such as the disruptive ML Hybrid Ensembling procedure for increasing the performance of the inspection system. Interoperable Digital System for Industry 4.0 deployments: The multivariate data from the final downscaled dual spectroscopic fusion analyser (and from any other relevant data source as discussed below), will be collected in a Data Management Platform (which will be adapted from the IRIS ‘Scadalytics’ software), consisting of the following layers: ■ Communication Interfaces and APIs layer for data integration and interoperability with the dual spectroscopic fusion analyser, and for receiving data from SCADA/ERP, PLC, other their party analysers & Industrial IoT devices; ■ Data processing layer performs data conditioning prior to its use by the data fusion layer. ■ Data fusion layer employs hybrid-ensemble modelling for implementing data fusion (ensuring a common architecture in relation to the data processing tools, whereby the hybrid-ensemble modelling has different roles in the application R&D and implementation road map).The most straightforward approach for implementing data fusion through hybrid ensembling involves identifying optimal pairings or tuples of instruments and machine learning methods within a specific ensemble architecture, in order to achieve both the most robust and accurate predictive algorithm. An instant evident advantage is a reduction in the number of observations (samples) required for developing models that are not prone to undergo overfitting. In fact, the lack of sufficient numbers of different samples (as many as required for building useful models) is the most frequent barrier to the wide use of ML-based spectroscopic analysers. The hybrid ensembling concept addresses the limitation of two extreme approaches: on the one hand, modelling the merged data by means of just one weakly optimal algorithm and, on the other hand, applying the machine learning ensemble approach to a dataset that involves limited information provided by just one type of spectroscopic analyser. The outputs from this layer can be either sent directly to the Scada, can be interfaced through a self-contained User Interface (providing information for improved decision making, sending alerts to operators, as well as using data historian about the process for improved process understanding, optimisation, predictions, & anomaly detection), or can be sent up to an upper layer of intelligence for use by predictive models. The collection and processing of multivariate data from the LIBS-Raman dual spectroscopic fusion analyser in an interoperable data management platform that can also fuse this data with other available univariate sensors from the processing line has the potential to move industrial processes, such as complex recycling, towards process automation through multivariate process control (MVPC), as well as ‘soft-sensors’ trained on historic process data. Industry cocreation and participation in the R&D process and the market validation process: Our application-oriented R&D approach will be driven by a cocreation process involving early adopters and stakeholders through 4 scoping sessions in the first 4 four months. These will engage ≥20 organisations from diverse industrial segments to gather input on system design, industrial requirements and KPIs, which will feed into iterative development and market segmentation. This will shape our Go-to-Market (GTM) strategy, targeting plastics recyclers and a first PVC recycling use case, with clear pathways for follow-on markets. Participants will also define the performance, operational and business KPIs, which will be validated during industrial trials. As such, in the second project phase, the VISUM LRS prototype will be installed and tested at Deceuninck’s large-scale PVC recycling facility in Diksmuide, Belgium (fig. 3). Thanks to the at-line work performed with them during REMADYL, Deceuninck identified suitable integration spots in their existing recycling facility for piloting the system, which would allow them to effectively sort more complex input streams and separate the material steams into fractions in compliance with legislation. They have subsequently signed a LOI to pilot the VISUM LRS prototype in their facilities during this EIC Transition project Trials will measure sorting performance against the defined KPIs, enabling impact assessment and system optimisation. These results will support the creation of Application Notes and Business Case Studies (that can be appropriately anonymised to not reveal any sensitive information). Crucially, the cocreation process (scoping sessions, industrial testing and validation process, and the demonstration of the innovation to early adopters) will guide and validate our scale-up roadmap for two implementation modes: ‘dynamic spot’ for conveyor-based industrial applications and ‘static spot’ for at-line inspection. This dual mode enhances versatility and wides market reach, with costing, production and deployment strategies informed directly by early adopters. Technical robustness of our AI-based system and techniques: The machine learning components of our system will be developed in full compliance with the EC’s AI Act and the High-Level Expert Group's guidelines on Trustworthy AI, which define key requirements around transparency, accountability, human oversight, robustness and data governance. In this context, the AI system will be “trustworthy-by-design” in two ways: 1) Quantitative chemical validation. As the core of the system is an optical chemical analyser, performance can be objectively/ quantitatively validated using traceable physical standards or application-specific benchmarks. Detection accuracy will be benchmarked against known concentrations of lead and phthalates in PVC samples, with standardised ground truth references used to evaluate and verify model predictions. 2) Robust ML methodology: We will use hybrid ensemble models that fuse LIBS and Raman data to improve generalisation and reduce overfitting risk. To ensure scientific rigour, model performance and reliability will be assessed through cross-validation and statistical significance tests such as Fisher-Pitman randomisation test. These approaches will allow us to evaluate the likelihood that that high performance is due to model quality rather than random variation in data. To ensure security, auditability, and transparency, all training data, model parameters, and evaluation results will be version-controlled and traceable. Human-in-the-loop mechanisms will be maintained throughout the development and deployment phases to guarantee interpretability and user control. How the project methodology complies with the ‘do no significant harm’ (DHSH) principle: Our project activities will not cause any significant harm to any of the 6 environmental objectives defined in Regulation (EU) 2020/852: 1) Climate change mitigation; 2) Climate change adaptation; 3) Sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources; 4) Transition to a circular economy; 5) Pollution prevention and control; 6) Protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems. From a technology engineering perspective, the system design and operation of the Visum LRS inspection system capitalises on common hardware, which will contribute to resource efficiency. Moreover, ultimate solution is primarily based on mature technologies (e.g. lasers, spectrometers, electronics) with low resource demands, long operational lifespan & low energy consumption, ensuring minimal environmental impact relative to the benefits delivered. Regarding waste recycling and our first PVC use case, the Visum LRS will prevent non-complaint PVC entering the market. This is critical, as the current consensus in the scientific community is that there is no safe level of exposure to lead (Pb), and particularly so for young children,, whereby cumulative childhood exposure can result in damage to the brain and nervous system, slowed growth, anaemia, hearing loss, and behavioural and learning problems. In adults, exposure can increase blood pressure and incidence of hypertension, decrease kidney function and reduce fertility. The device will also be an enabler for complaint PVC recycling (avoiding incineration and landfill routes) to further enhance the circularity of the PVC industry, reducing environmental impact. According to Deceuninck, recycling PVC leads to 90% less energy consumption that producing virgin raw material. Gender dimension: We will adopt a ‘gendered innovation’ approach (Subtask 7.2.3) to integrate sex, gender, and intersectional analysis into our research, adding dimensions that might otherwise be overlooked. We will ensure equitable representation of female stakeholders and end-users in co-design and co-creation, from requirement definition to industrial trials and system assessment. Dissemination and Exploitation outputs will use gender-inclusive language and imagery, reflecting the roles and interests of both men and women, e.g. policies such as LS restrictions in PVC recycling address health risks that disproportionately affect women

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Provide a comprehensive analysis of the current state of the art, identify gaps, and explain how this project advances beyond existing knowledge.
Write the State of the art and innovation section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.2 Methodology Overall methodology. Underpinning concepts, Models & Assumptions: Our methodology centres on three tracks (figure 3): 1) sensor development and scaling- both photonic and non-optical (to TRL7), 2) multimodal integration designed around 3 industrial use cases (TRL4-5), and 3) readiness for future industrial deployment (productisation roadmaps, training, business case development, securing IP assets and standardisation). Track 1 centres on scaling the technology: on one side, the advanced photonic sensors (i.e. compact inline ICL/QCL MIR analyser, intelligent SERS sensing platform, fully integrated indium gallium arsenide (InGaAs) single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) sensor with on-chip photon count accumulation) and non-optical chemical sensing array platform will each be matured to TRL7 as standalone technologies. On the other side, the current multirange spectroscopy, hybrid ensembling enabled test bed platform will be scaled into an industrial multimodal integration platform, prepared for enabling the TRL4-5 demonstrations. Track 2 involves working with the use cases (pharmaceuticals bioprocessing, plastics recycling and steelmaking) in early mapping of the multimodal sensing solutions against the complex monitoring requirements to define the specifications and contexts of each case. These will inform the designs for the multimodal configurations to be implemented once the sensing modules (photonic and non-optical) are ready. The multimodal integration platform will then be leveraged for configuring, integrating, testing and validating the multimodal solutions over 2 iterative loops: one at lab scale (TRL4) and, based on the results, we will deploy and validate the multimodal solutions in industrially relevant environments (TRL5) at the facilities of each UC owner. Track 3 will ensure that the frameworks are in place for a post-project scale up and productisation, skills development, business planning, IP protection and compliance. This will support a competitive time-to-market (TTM) and readiness for industrial adoption, while contributing to standardisation and wider ecosystem uptake. TRACK ONE: DEVELOPMENT OF THE SENSOR TECHNOLOGIES & INTEGRATION PLATFORM (TO TRL7) Advanced photonic sensor developments QCL/ICL MIR: ALPES has developed in-house and produces the “GLIDER”(https://www.alpeslasers.ch/glider/). The current version of the GLIDER is designed for multi-laser operation with wide spectral tuneability. It has a modular design and consists of a QCL gain chip, a grating-tuned extended optical cavity in Littrow configuration, laser drive electronics, a temperature controller, and a graphical user interface software. The GLIDER aims compatibility with the ALPES line of Broad Gain QCLs which tune over up to 25% of their central wavelength. The QCL /ICL – GLIDER will be designed to maximize the tuning range of the assembly. However, it is not currently optimised for ICL broadgain devices. The key challenge here will be the difference in polarization between QCLs and ICLs. An optical assembly will be required to overcome this issue. A set of 1 ICL and 3 QCLs will be integrated in the GLIDER, in order to produce an ultra-wide spectral tuning device, capable of a tuning range of over 1200 cm-1 in total. The multi-laser setup is selected in order to reduce device complexity, cost & size. Further to the development of the laser assembly, ALPES will develop the custom-designed driving electronics for its devices, designed to work with the MIR sensing tool. Regarding the sensing modality, the proposed technical approach integrates advanced mid-infrared (MIR) sources, substrate-integrated hollow waveguides (iHWGs), and data fusion into a single sensing platform. At the photonic level, iHWGs will act as miniaturised gas cells and optical conduits. Tailored iHWG geometries will be developed to ensure durability and sensitivity under corrosive atmospheres, high humidity, and high temperatures. Complementarily, the MIR optical architecture will incorporate with with chemical sensor arrays in a shared manifold, guaranteeing correlated and orthogonal data streams. Preprocessing and chemometric pipelines will provide real-time baseline correction, drift compensation, and predictive analytics, enabling robust operation across complex industrial matrices. SERS: The proposed technical approach integrates next-generation SERS substrates, advanced optical modules, and AI-driven analytics into a single industrial sensing platform. On the substrates side, we will develop SHINERS core-shell nanoparticles and/or hybrid plasmonic–dielectric meta surfaces to achieve reproducible, high-intensity electromagnetic hotspots with enhanced durability under thermal, chemical, and mechanical stress. Complementarily, the optical architecture will incorporate dual-wavelength excitation, confocal fibre-coupled probes, time gated and high-sensitivity cooled detectors, ensuring¡ robust signal collection and effective fluorescence suppression in complex industrial matrices. These innovations will be integrated into modular, ruggedized systems suitable for in-line monitoring of steel, bioreactor, and recycled plastic streams. An edge AI analytical layer will perform real-time spectral unmixing, baseline correction and predictive process control, delivering quantitative, reproducible, and actionable molecular fingerprints. This convergent strategy advances SERS from sensitive laboratory demonstrations to industrial-grade, TRL6–7 deployable systems, setting a new benchmark for high-performance, reliable, and scalable optical sensing in harsh environments. InGaAs SPAD camera module with on-chip processing: The project applies an integrated approach combining pixel and sensor array design and fabrication, electronics design, packaging, process development, and experimental validation. Pixel design and fabrication: Individual InGaAs SPAD pixels will be optimized for low dark count rate (DCR), i.e. noise, and high photon detection efficiency (PDE) via numerical simulations. Pixel contact metallizations will be performed after receiving wafers. Assumption: validated pixel structures and fabrication recipes from prior prototypes can be adapted. Challenge: balancing sensitivity, noise, and afterpulsing while maintaining manufacturability. Pixel array architecture: A 192×128-pixel array with 25 µm pitch will be developed. Assumption: array scaling does not introduce prohibitive crosstalk or afterpulsing. Challenge: managing uniformity, yield, and power distribution across the full array. Electronics & software: Printed circuit board (PCB), firmware, and user interface (UI) will be developed with modular design and standard electronics design automation (EDA) tools. Assumption: ROIC specifications are stable. Challenge: combining high-speed data acquisition with a user-friendly interface. Packaging: A thermo-electric cooled, hermetically sealed package will be built using thermal and vacuum simulations before prototyping. Assumption: commercial thermo-electric cooling (TEC)/sealing solutions can be adapted. Challenge: ensuring compactness while maintaining thermal stability. ROIC testing: ROICs will be diced, bonded, powered up, and validated with emulated SPAD signals. Assumption: foundry ROICs have an acceptable baseline yield. Challenge: minimizing die loss and resolving functional issues early. 3D integration: Under bump metallization (UBM), indium bumping, and flip-chip bonding will be optimized iteratively on dummy wafers before transfer to real devices. Assumption: process scalability is achievable. Challenge: precise alignment and yield reproducibility. Hybridization & characterization: Final ROIC–SPAD bonding will create hybridized chips, followed by camera tests under controlled illumination to measure DCR, PDE, and resolution, and secondary noise mechanisms such as afterpulsing. Assumption: standard calibration methods are sufficient. Challenge: achieving uniform array performance in hybridized chips. Non-optical sensing developments Chemical sensor arrays: In PhotonX, gas sensor arrays are introduced as a complementary modality that expands the scope and applicability of the advanced photonic sensors in a multimodal approach. While MIR, Raman SERS and SPAD provide high-resolution, molecule-specific and time-resolved measurements, each technology has intrinsic limits in terms of selectivity, coverage or sensitivity to complex mixtures. Chemosensors address this gap by offering broad, pattern-based detection of volatile organic compounds and trace gases that cannot be fully resolved by photonic approaches alone. With MIR spectroscopy, chemosensors enables multi-dimensional monitoring of the gas headspace by adding sensitivity to trace-level VOC mixtures in the ppb–low ppm range, which often fall below MIR quantification thresholds or lack strong IR activity. MIR delivers molecule-specific, quantitative information for well-defined analytes such as CO, CO₂, H₂O and selected VOC markers, as well as environmental parameters (temperature and humidity). These parameters can be used in real time to stabilise chemosensors performance, compensating for baseline drift, cross-sensitivities, and ageing effects. In turn, the pattern-based detection of chemosensors arrays captures low-level and complex mixtures not quantifiable by MIR alone. Fusing both datasets increases robustness and improves effective limits of detection by an estimated 20–30% over stand-alone spectroscopic sensors, while enhancing classification accuracy under dynamic process conditions. With Raman SERS, the chemosensors extend detection beyond the targeted fingerprints retrieved by advanced substrates (e.g. SHINERS, metasurfaces). While SERS offers selective, quantitative identification of contaminants or process markers, chemosensors and, particularly MOX sensors, contributes a global chemical profile at sub-ppm levels. This dual layer ensures that Raman’s precision is contextualized within a broader view of the gas-phase environment, reducing false negatives and enabling early anomaly detection in complex bioreactors, recycling or steelmaking contexts. Figure 5: Different sensor modules available at JLM a,b) module with research level triple MOX sensor, c,d) sensor modules with commercial gas sensors. Multimodal integration platform IRIS will scale up its AI-driven multirange spectroscopic testbed platform (known as the VISUM Fusion) and extend it to an industrial multimodal integration platform. Fig 6- High level schematic of the multimodal integration full stack: 1) Multimodal configurator to design the sensing modality templates for the 2) industrial multimodal devices, which is supported by 3) AI-driven Data Fusion (Hybrid Emsembling Machine Learning) and 4) AI analytics tools (predictive engine) From figure 6, it can be seen that this framework consists of the multimodal configurator all the common elements (power supply, embedded computer, dedicated software, user interface, communication module), plus a ‘shared suite’ of the specific sensors/spectrometers, the respective light sources, and the suitable probes, capable of delivering the following sensing techniques: VIS; NIR; SWIR; MWIR; Raman; LIBS; Fluorescence; and MV. It is also ready with the readout electronics for non-optical probes. The platform also enables seamless data fusion, to obtain dramatic improvements in precision, accuracy, and limit of detection, and it does so based on a new Hybrid Ensemble paradigm. This setup is the ideal framework to be scaled up in PhotonX and will integrate into its spectroscopic sensing modalities the necessary hardware components for the Novel QLC MIR, the SERs and the InGaAs SPAD camera module with on-chip photon count accumulation, along with e-noses non-optical elements. It will act as a Multimodal Configurator that essentially capitalises on opportunities for hardware-level fusion of sensing modalities (sharing optical paths, illumination sources, detectors, and housing where feasible) to reduce system footprint, enhance robustness, and lower costs while maintaining or improving measurement performance. This flexible configurator manages the selection and configuration of complementary optical and non-optical techniques to deliver the required sensing capabilities for a given use case. It will be used and validated in the 3 Use Cases for allowing rapid testing with a variety of sensing techniques to save time in the experimental phase, and to allow the most suitable fusion of techniques to be identified. This will provide the template for ‘downscaling’ (figure 7) to a compact and affordable industrial inline analytical device that benefits from the common hardware compatible (‘Fusion Core’) of the Visum Fusion. To further extend the monitoring capabilities, data provided by off-the-shelf compatible univariate sensors can be fused with the spectroscopy and imaging data and relating them to quality parameters of the production and product performance to provide a more robust mathematical model, automatic control of processes, reducing defects and improving yields, reducing time, waste and energy consumption, and for predictive maintenance. The multivariate data from the final downscaled analytical device, and from any other relevant data source will be collected in the Scadalytics Data Management Platform (developed by IRIS and which will be adapted and scaled as the intelligent software for the multimodal stack). It consists of the following layers: Communication Interfaces and APIs layer: designed for data integration and interoperability not only within the frame of the multimodal sensing platform, but can also receive data from SCADA/ERP, PLC, from other analysers, Industrial IoT devices, whereby API-ready interfaces to ensure seamless integration with existing plant information and control systems, supporting interoperability and adherence to industrial standards. A data processing layer performs data conditioning prior to its use by the data fusion layer. It manages, fuses, and analyses heterogeneous data streams through hybrid AI and enabling interoperability with industrial systems (SCADA, etc) and other external information systems (such as Digital Product Passports). Likewise, the data processing layer allows the integration of third parties algorithms and signal quality criteria for dedicated tasks linked to specific sensors (for example, base-line corrections, noise suppression or sensor response calibrations) to take advantage of available prior knowledge, either empirical or first-principle-based. At the core of the data fusion layer, a groundbreaking advancement is the hybrid-ensemble modelling for implementing data fusion (it is noteworthy that Scadalytics and VisumFusion share a common architecture in relation to the data processing tools, while they have different roles in the application development and implementation road map). The most straightforward approach for implementing data fusion through hybrid ensembling involves identifying optimal pairings or tuples of instruments and machine learning methods within a specific ensemble architecture, in order to achieve both the most robust and accurate predictive algorithm. An instant evident advantage is a reduction in the number of observations (samples) required for developing models that are not prone to undergo overfitting. In fact, the lack of sufficient numbers of different samples (as many as required for building useful models) is the most frequent barrier to the wide use of machine-learning-based spectroscopic analysers. The hybrid ensembling concept addresses the limitation of two extreme approaches: on the one hand, modelling the merged data by means of just one weakly optimal algorithm and, on the other hand, applying the machine learning ensemble approach to a dataset that involves limited information provided by just one type of spectroscopic analyser. The outputs from this layer can be either sent directly to the Scada, can be interfaced through a self-contained User Interface (providing information for improved decision making, sending alerts to operators, as well as using data historian about the process for improved process understanding, optimisation, predictions, & anomaly detection), or can be sent up to an upper layer of intelligence for use by predictive models. The Decision Layer focuses on delivering actionable intelligence back to operators and control systems, enabling informed, human-centric decision-making and, where appropriate, closed-loop control. It leverages AI-driven predictions and simulations to support methodologies such as Quality by Design (QbD), SSbD, and Model Predictive Control (MPC). In cases where higher-fidelity process simulations are valuable, the Decision Layer can be implemented within a Digital Twin framework, providing dynamic, real-time synchronisation between the physical process and its virtual counterpart for scenario planning, optimisation, and resilience. We will ensure that the AI developments comply with EU Artificial Intelligence Act, upholding the principles of transparency, accountability, and human oversight. Our AI systems will be: Lawful, in full compliance with applicable EU legislation; Ethical, following the Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, with attention to data privacy, transparency, fairness, and bias mitigation; Robust and reliable, through rigorous scenario testing, monitoring for unexpected behaviours, and incorporation of hybrid human-AI oversight where needed; Explainable, using interpretable models and providing context, limitations, and access to relevant training data to support user understanding and trust. TRACK 2: MULTIMODAL INTEGRATION DESIGNED AROUND INDUSTRIAL USE CASES (TRL4-5) Design of multimodal solutions for Use Cases: Track 2 focuses on translating the scaled sensing technologies into multimodal configurations designed around three industrial use cases: pharmaceuticals bioprocessing, plastics recycling, and steelmaking. As such building on the sensor developments and the integration framework from Track 1, we will undertake a structured design process to configure multimodal solutions tailored to the monitoring challenges in the three UCs. This involves early co-definition of requirements with UC owners (process parameters, contaminants, dynamic ranges, environmental constraints), mapping them against the capabilities of the photonic and non-optical sensing modalities, to identify the most effective combinations of photonic and non-optical sensing, ensuring that the platform addresses complexity, variability, and reliability under industrial conditions. The designs will define the specific multimodal sensor pairings, hardware interfaces, and AI-driven data fusion pipelines that will then be implemented and validated in the UCs at TRL4–5. This track will leverage the Industrial Multimodal Integration Platform (physics + signal + data fusion) for configuring, deploying, and operating the multimodal solutions. Prototypes from each of the sensing modalities will be temporarily ‘emsembled’ (like a ‘mecano’)- for servicing the distinct multimodal configurations of the different UCs. It will deliver specifications and KPIs, guide the design of the multimodal configurations, and validate them through an iterative loop of laboratory testing (TRL4) followed by installation and operation in industrial environments (TRL5). This iterative TRL4 to TRL5 validation loop reduces technical and adoption risks by first derisking performance in controlled lab conditions before demonstrating robustness, reliability, and value under real industrial operating environments. These UCs will serve to validate both the intrinsic performance metrics (e.g., resolution, sensitivity, stability, signal-to-noise ratio) and extrinsic metrics (e.g., robustness, cross-sensitivity, environmental stability) of the technological innovations, as well as to demonstrate the business, efficiency, sustainability, and societal benefits in each case. Design of multimodal solutions for Use Cases The three Use Case demonstrators are discussed in turn: Use Case 1- Pharmaceutical Bioprocessing (MyBiotech and USAAR): MyBiotech is a CDMO (Contract Development and Manufacturing Organization) providing end-to-end development and manufacturing services for biotech and pharma. It specialises in nanopharmaceutical development, production, and characterisation, supported by proprietary continuous manufacturing technologies (flow reactors, impinging jets, top-down size reduction) and a GMP-compliant facility. MyB also works on downprocessing of microorganisms and the optimisation of key enabling technologies. USAAR is pioneering advanced biocatalysts and bioprocesses and performs cutting-edge research in metabolic engineering, systems biology, and bioprocess development and optimization, driving innovations in the sustainable production of chemicals, materials, food, drugs, and therapeutics, with a focus on valorising renewable resources, industrial side streams, and waste, supported by state-of-the-art bioanalytical platforms and fermentation systems ranging from microreactors to 15 L bioreactors. Soil bacteria of the genus Streptomyces synthesize a rich spectrum of natural products, including more than 75% of all industrial antibiotics. Streptomyces rimosus and Streptomyces albus are established hosts for the heterologous production of oxytetracycline (OTC), a broad-spectrum antibiotic of the tetracycline family and among the most prescribed antibiotics in the world (Beganovic et al, 2023). The compound inhibits pathogenic gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. Meanwhile, it is also the precursor for the synthesis of Doxycycline which is in the EMAs list of critical medicines (https://www.ema.europa.eu/). Typically, the biosynthesis of secondary metabolites is highly complex and tightly regulated by the microbe, which makes their economic production challenging. Additionally, the fermentation process is dependent on costly nutrients that unfavourably compete with human nutrition (e.g. glucose, starch). For PhotonX, we envision a more sustainable, nutrient-limited fed-batch or semi-continuous process based on renewable feedstocks (i.e. lignocellulosic biomass, lignin and/or seaweed) in which critical metabolic and process parameters are monitored online and integrated into a comprehensive process model. The use of complex and often inhibitory biomass waste streams as carbon source necessitates careful monitoring and dosing of the contained compounds, especially in biopharmaceutical applications. Likewise, the accumulation of the target antibiotic during production poses an increasing metabolic burden to the cell eventually leading to growth arrest and production limitations. Therefore, critical metabolic and process parameters i.e. carbon substrate concentrations, intracellular metabolite levels (e.g. Acetyl-CoA, ATP or NADPH), anorganic ions and other trace elements (ammonium, phosphate, vitamins, trace metals), cell density, pH and dissolved oxygen levels, as well as secreted by-product and OTC titers are continuously monitored. The collected sensor data is integrated into a quantitative online process model that allows for the control and automatisation of the production process based on nutrient availability and cell fitness. Sensor calibration and process integration will be first established by USAAR at lab-scale (1 L) and later transferred to project partner MyBiotech for scale-up to pilot-scale production (15 L). Integration of advanced photonics and sensing technology in combination with AI at the academic and industrial level will demonstrate, facilitate and smoothen the transfer from R&D level to industrial environment of biopharmaceutical manufacturing. Today higher OTC levels are achieved by cultivating mutagenized Streptomyces producer strains in simple batch processes using pure sugars as carbon source and nutrient limitations. Process parameters like temperature, pH and dissolved oxygen concentration are monitored using suitable electrodes. Broth from the process is regularly sampled and the obtained supernatants are analysed for relevant substrate and (by-)product concentrations to follow the process’ progress by labour intensive techniques like HPLC. Process control or automatization based on online monitored compound levels or biochemical parameters is not common. BSOTA: We estimate the following potential: +50% titer, 25% rate and +10% yield compared to the SOTA at lab-scale (Reference: 100 mg/L, 1 mg/L/h, and 10 mg/g, respectively), an overall reduction of process costs by -10% with use of agricultural and forest-industry side streams, a general improved process control and robustness, buffering batch-to-batch variations in raw material supply thereby enabling the use of cheaper and more complex feedstocks. Features of the novel approach: Competitive European production of antibiotics from local biobased feedstocks. Lower dependency on imports to avoid medicine shortages. Sustainable production from renewables, with lower carbon footprint and less contaminated wastewater. Contribute to Critical Medicines Act, especially in enabling antibiotic production in Europe by supporting fermentative production, ensuring the availability of antibiotics. Contribute to Strategic approach to pharmaceuticals in the environment, if technology can be used to monitor oxytetracycline in wastewater. EMAs list of critical medicines. Bioprocessing Monitoring: Chemical Sensor Array (CSA) + SERS + SPAD: MIR (ATR probe) will provide reliable detection/quantification of low‑level process major metabolites, nutrients and by-product markers in the liquid/solid phase (e.g., glucose, organic acids, proteins (H₂S exemplar) with improves accuracy and stable operation. Chemical Sensor Array (CSA) allows monitoring of the headspace of the bioreactors, detecting volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from microbial metabolism, as an early-warning system for contamination or microbial stress. SERS will add ultra-sensitive detection of the final product; oxytetracycline (down to µM or nM levels), even in early formation stages, via tailored substrates (i.e. silver nanostructures). SPAD camera enables visual inspection in the water spectral window (around 1100 nm) because SWIR is less prone to scattering in turbid environments as well as to pass through thin biofilms. Suitable for detecting macroscopic heterogeneities that could result from growing milieu artefacts. SERS will add ultra-sensitive detection of the final product; oxytetracycline (down to µM or nM levels), even in early formation stages, via tailored substrates (i.e. silver nanostructures). SPAD camera enables time-resolved fluorescence for live monitoring of the microbial metabolic state, detecting the fluorescence of specific metabolites (i.e.NAD/FAD) in the cell culture. The multimodal solution will be coupled with already existing sensors in bioreactors (i.e. temperature probes, electrochemical and pH sensors) for enriching the data fusion layer. Use Case 2 –Plastics Recycling (Van Werven): Van Werven Plastic Recycling recycles 180,000 tonnes rigid plastic across 7 European locations. The location in Biddinghuizen is the largest location, recycling 80,000 tonnes of rigid plastics per year. At this facility, building and demolition plastic waste and mixed plastics from recycling centres are collected and sorted in more than 20 categories, based on polymer type, colour, additive content and properties. The sorted plastics are grinded, washed, dried and packaged, so they can be used in plastic conversion processes. During the sorting process for mixed plastic waste, 6-8 workers manually pick different types of plastic from a transport belt. Plastics contain a wide range of additives, such as flame retardants, coloring agents with a certain chemical composition, plasticizers, antioxidants, UV- stabilizers and so on. These additives have been used for years, but regulations, based on the current knowledge on how damaging additives can be for human health and the environment, now prohibit the use in plastics. Since recycled plastics are used to make new products, they recycled plastics have to comply to current regulation (e.g. REACH and POP). Therefore, one needs to remove plastics that contain prohibited additives (‘legacy additives’) as soon as possible. In the Van Werven recycling process, removal should happen in the first step: the sorting of plastics. Currently, this is done by human operators (see the picture above). Human eyes can differentiate between some types of plastic, but it is very difficult to detect additives. The technology used in this project may help to differentiate between the desired plastics to be recycled, and undesired ones containing legacy additives. In the table below, examples of legacy additives are shown, with the level that is currently allowed in plastic raw materials. 1.2 Methodology Overall methodology. Underpinning concepts, Models & Assumptions Our project aims to decarbonize the EU steel sector by combining three technological approaches that we will investigate over 5 strategically linked and integrated research and innovation blocks: BLOCK 1. Produce high quality, biobased reducing agents for Low-CO2 iron and steelmaking: Our research activity will focus on transforming biowaste streams into valuable products, maximizing the valorisation of carbon to produce biogenic reducing agents usable for the decarbonization of the steel sector. As inorganic nutrients like P, K, etc. are harmful for the steelmaking industry, we will focus on hydrometallurgical processes that also allow the full recovery of the inorganic nutrients to make them usable in agriculture. The quality and usability of biogenic reducing agents will be tested in lab and pilot steelmaking furnaces. BLOCK 2. Improve circularity in steelmaking by reusing residual streams for new green steel processes: We will approach different circularity solutions aimed at using steelmaking residues as feedstock for the production of new biobased reducing agents. Particularly, slag obtained by Si-Mn-Fe furnaces (ERA, SINTEF) will be tested as a catalyst in the methane cracking unit (REC) with a view to obtaining a combined effect: produce hydrogen for DRI and obtain a C-deposition on the slag that can be used as a reducing agent in the Si-Mn production process. Moreover, slag from Scrap EAF (CELSA) will be sent to REC and TEC to be compacted in mixtures with bioanthracite to produce bioanthracite granules and briquettes with low dust and high density, adequate pH, and optimised for our use in EAF. BLOCK 3. Increase the energy efficiency of steelmaking by maximizing the waste heat recovery and increasing the rate of bioenergy use: We will perform a specific energy assessment and modelling study. Particularly, our activity will focus on a future biobased steelmaking, where pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment processes (HTT, SCWG) will be installed within iron and steelmaking sites, whereby the thermal energy required could be obtained by reusing the waste heat of DRI, EAF, Sintering and BFs. With this solution, the excess energy from pyrolysis and the process gases could be used directly for steelmaking, as well as for producing renewable bioelectricity. This research will be carried out under the leadership of POLI, taking energy data from both pilot tests performed (CEA, REC, AMMR), and the existing steelmaking processes run by steel industries involved in our consortium (CELSA, AMMR, ERA). All the data will be elaborated by modelling tools for identifying the different integrated energy systems, for determining the plants integration strategy, emission savings’ potential, & economic benefits of the proposed solution. BLOCK 4. Digitalisation and Intelligent Process Control (HoloSteel): We will implement a dedicated digital intelligence layer (HoloSteel) to support the integration and stability of the bio-based steelmaking pathways. HoloSteel will provide real-time monitoring, process simulation, and decision support across multiple steelmaking routes. By combining Process Analytical Technologies (PAT), Quality by Design (QbD), and a modular agent-based digital twin architecture, HoloSteel will deliver system-wide predictability and adaptive control, especially under variable feedstock conditions. All digital assets will be FAIR-compliant, enabling structured data management, interoperability with existing plant systems, and integration with DIGITAL-61 actions. This digital layer will be critical for increasing the reliability, transparency, and scalability of low-CO₂ steelmaking operations. BLOCK 5. Industry 5.0, Skills, and Business Case: To ensure deployment readiness and long-term sustainability, we will integrate Industry 5.0 principles into the technology scale-up process. This includes the co-creation of new operational roles and training materials to address the emerging skills gap in digital and green steelmaking environments (towards Operator 5.0). We will also build out a quantified business case and exploitation roadmap, including techno-economic analysis and lifecycle costing for the biobased reductants, biohydrogen, and circular integration strategies. This block will also include coordination with European Social Fund Plus (ESF+) initiatives to support future upskilling programmes. We will also ensure alignment with European industrial and policy roadmaps (e.g. Clean Steel Partnership, Net-Zero Industry Act), ensuring that our innovations are positioned for investment-readiness and real-world adoption. Our project focuses on using biowaste streams (sludge, organic fraction of municipal waste, agri-food industry residues) to produce biobased reducing agents. These materials are abundant biogenic sources available and, as opposed to solid biomass, they currently have a very low or negative cost/value. However, the char obtained from these biowastes contains high amounts of inorganics, such as P, K, Mg etc, harmful for the steel sector. At the same time, these elements are of high value for agriculture. The main goal of this block is to demonstrate the technical feasibility of an integrated process capable of transforming the biogenic carbon contained in biowastes, producing bio-anthracite and H2-rich gas, ensuring the extraction and recovery of inorganic elements, such as phosphorus, magnesium, iron. Our solution consists of an integrated process (figure 1) as follows: a fast hydrothermal treatment (Fast HTT), at high heating rate and low residence time, to replace the drying process and transform biowaste into two materials: a volatile carbon-rich solid called hydrochar (55-60% of initial carbon), and a liquid phase (30-35% of the initial carbon in a soluble form). A chemical leaching step is performed on the hydrochar to extract >99% of the Ca, Mg and P, thereby reducing ash content in the solid. After leaching, the obtained low-ash hydrochar is upgraded by slow pyrolysis to obtain a low-volatile, carbon-rich high value bioanthracite. In the event of still high concentration of inorganics, such as Al, Fe, Si, slow pyrolysis will be performed with the use of alkali additives. The use of alkali additives solubilises the inorganic compounds present in the solid after pyrolysis which can be removed by a bio-anthracite washing step and recovered to produce chemicals (flocculating agents and P-rich fertiliser). On the liquid phases, SWCG and AD are performed to turn all the organic carbon contained in the aqueous phase into a H2 rich gas (called SCWG gas). Both these products - the gas and the bioanthracite - are usable as biogenic reducing agents in the DRI and EAF process routes, decarbonizing the steel sector. The extracted inorganics are recovered by precipitation and reused for other industrial purposes in a circular model. In addition to its role as a reducing agent in DRI and EAF processes, the H2 rich gas can serve as a clean alternative to natural gas as fuel in burners of reheating furnaces, thus contributing to a reduction in fossil fuel consumption. Finally, a novel green-hydrogen generation process is proposed, tested and developed by REC and POLI in the H2STEEL project. The process is based on using the bioanthracite as a catalyst in a new methane catalytic pyrolyser that transforms methane & biomethane from the AD process into green hydrogen. High quality C-enriched bioanthracite is obtained as a bio-product suitable for use in EAF and other steelmaking processes. Methodology and approach in producing biogenic reducing agents at TRL 4. We will first optimize the process in the laboratory at TRL4 in a research-industry partnership: our industry partners will provide us with relevant information on the quality of biogenic reducing agents to be achieved, and the market requirements. This activity will be based, first on tests in small scale units of 300-2000 ml capacity for HTT and SCWG tests and on small scale pyrolysis and chemical leaching batch reactors (2 kg capacity). The tests will enable us to identify the most promising feedstocks to be used at industrial scale and will also define the optimized process conditions to ensure quality, sustainability and the economic feasibility of the process. We will test the proposed process on a variety of biowaste residues presenting different properties. This approach will contribute to achieving high quality of the final products, and to optimize the whole process in terms of economic and environmental sustainability (assisted by the HoloSteel modular digital intelligence layer). The development and optimization of the processes to produce biogenic reducing agents will be performed using pilot units available at REC & CEA that will enable 5 biowaste types to be processed under different process conditions for validating the processes and the quality of the products. These pilot units will enable us to produce reliable quantities of green gas and bio-anthracite, which will then be processed and tested by TEC and SIN as reducing agents in steel and ironmaking processes. Methodology and approach in producing biogenic reducing agents at TRL 4-6: One of the main obstacles for the validation of coal alternatives in steelmaking is the large quantities needed by steel industries in order to perform pilot tests in their furnaces; the pilot scale furnaces that we will use (AMMR, CELSA, ERA) in NEXTSTEEL will require >100kg of bioanthracite for not only demonstrating the quality at TRL6, but also for identifying any barrier with regard to handling, injection, and emission aspects. The existing and newly constructed facilities that we will use for producing the innovative biobased reductants in real environments are listed below. Fast HTT continuous reactor of >250 kg/h
